column5: Digital surrogate of sculptural relief depicting 6 Scythians with various instruments performing at bacchanalia. There is a column with a Corinthian capital to the left and the face of the figure on the far right seems to have been removed.

column5: Digital surrogate of metalwork depicting a golden tiger in a figurative manner with its claws depicted along the bottom and abstract fur on its body. It appears to have faces of previous prey depicted on the body. The lion holds an animal in its front paw and is seen devouring it.

column3: Belt Buckle with Paired Felines Attacking Ibexes
column5: Digital surrogate of metalwork depicting classic twisting animal motifs and interlace. Two felines are on the bottom, twisting around in a mirror image of one another to bite into the necks of two mirrored ibexes, whose antlers have decorative exterior designs and terminate at the ibexes back legs featured above them.

column5: Digital surrogate of metalwork depicting three ibexes in a woodland, two standing in a side profile with their faces pointing forward and the third from a frontal view. Their curved antlers interweave with the branches around them and the two on the sides both have a leg lifted into the branches as well.

column5: Digital surrogate of metalwork depicting an accessory for a bow. It shows concentric lines marking the boundaries of the different sections with concentric, interweaving geometric designs within.

column5: Digital surrogate of metalwork, this item is part of a belt buckle. It has five sunken panels, two on the bottom, two on the long shaft, and one for the top curved section. Small knobs can be seen on the exterior of these panels. In the middle of the panels are abstract scenes of leaves, scrollwork, and possibly birds or some other such animal heads. The spring/pin is missing from this item, and the two rings on the side would have been for a belt loop.
column17: As part of the Met's Open Access policy, you can freely copy, modify and distribute this image, even for commercial purposes.

column5: Digital surrogate of Sarmatian ewelry. It is a single earring made of gold with 3 teardrop-shaped sardonyx gems at the top with triangular gold prongs holding them in place. Around the gems is braided wire terminating in a gold disc at the tips. Below the gems are lines of braided gold with spiral shapes on either end. Chainlink hangs from the earring terminating in twists.

column5: Digital surrogate of text on paper, depicts the Sogdian language describing the Manichaean religion. Featured are a few sacred figures decorated in red. Pieces of the text are missing.

column5: Digital surrogate of bronze metalwork depicting a figural lion with only holes for the eyes, spikes representing a mane along the neck and head, spikes for claws, and spiral patterns on the rear and chest of the animal.  There is some linework on the torso and on the neck, and there is a ring for some kind of attachment connecting the tail and neck.

column3: Roundel with a horned animal, lions, and griffins
column5: Digital surrogate of metal and stonework, this piece is a roundel with turquoise inlay and gold leaf. Turqoise lines the exterior band in semi-circles and on an inner band separating two larger bands where animals are depicted. These animals are figural and seem to represent lions and griffins with turqoise inlay that would have gone on their bodies, eyes, chests, and ears, as well as their hooves/paws. Their legs are on the outside represented by a spiraled cone with a teardrop shape for the hooves/paws. The animal in the middle of the roundel is curled back on itself and has two horns pointing forwards, with similar turqoise inlay patterns to the other animals.

column5: Digital surrogate of metalwork, this item is a gold pendant featuring a reclining horse with chainlinks attached to its mane terminating in carnelian gems with gold discs attached to them. The body of the horse is textured in debossed dots, and there seems to have been some previous attachment on the bottom and rear of the horse.

column5: Digital surrogate of metalwork, this plaque depicts a creature in some kind of landscape made up of embossed triangles and squares. There is a radial of these triangles around the neck of the creature. The embossed creature has hooves, a curled tail at the end, antlers, and the head is looking backwards with its mouth open. There are two curved lines protruding from its back and diagonal lines connect its torso to its back legs.

column5: Digital surrogate of metalwork, this item seems to depict an Eurasian elk in gold. The animal holds its held aloft, standing tall, with its antlers spreading behind it over its back. This would have been used as a dress ornament, which would likely have been attached directly to the dress.

column3: Child's Coat with Ducks in Pearl Medallions
column5: Digital surrogate of textile. This item is a richly woven coat is woven in five colours, the base being in red with green and blue roundels featuring twin ducks in reds, yellows, blues, and whites. Plus-sign shaped embellishments in red, blue, and yellow decorate the space between. The outer coat is made of silk, and the pearl roundels are characteristic of Sogdian silks. The inner lining is from China and is made of twill damask with a floral pattern. This was likely made for a Tibetan prince.
